petitioner_background: The petitioner is a martial arts athlete who competes in the traditional karate discipline and has also served as an instructor in karate and Krav Maga. She sought classification as an individual of extraordinary ability under EB-1A (INA § 203(b)(1)(A)). She intends to continue her career as a competitive athlete and martial arts instructor in the United States. Her competition record spans from 2003 to 2014, with references to events in multiple countries.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted a letter from the founder/chairman of an organization listing 26 competitions in which she competed between 2003 and 2014, with a total of 46 gold, silver, and bronze medals. The letter identified competitions as 'National' and stated they were 'nationally or internationally recognized' because regulated by the World Traditional Karate Federation (WTKF). Competitions included events identified as various 'Cup' tournaments. She also submitted a letter from WTKF stating she received medals in three different Championships (three silver, three bronze, one gold), three medals at a Championship event, and four medals at an 'International Championship' event. Additionally, she submitted images of 14 'international medals' and certificates including a certificate from a traditional karate federation indicating participation in the 13th championship in traditional karate in 2003, and images of two medals she claimed were from a 2011 Championship.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO disagreed with the Director's determination that the initial evidence was sufficient. The letter lacked pertinent information such as dates of competitions and information regarding their significance. The record did not include supporting evidence such as official competition results, award certificates, or medals received in the events, or information regarding the specific competitions. The evidence was internally inconsistent: her 2003 medals appeared to be from the 1st Cup, not the event identified in the certificate; she claimed six medals from a 2005 Championship but the medals indicated they were from a different championship, and it was unlikely one athlete could receive multiple medals (gold, silver, and bronze) in the same individual kumite event without explanation; the medals she claimed were from a 2011 Championship actually indicated on their face they were from the '4th Traditional Karate Tournament' and were awarded for third and fourth place finishes. The AAO held that it is not presumed that every karate competition drawing from a national or international field of competitors awards nationally or internationally recognized prizes. The petitioner must resolve inconsistencies with independent, objective evidence per Matter of Ho.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner claimed eligibility based on membership in an organization, the WTKF, and the World Shotokan Karate Federation (WSKF), stating these associations only grant membership to the top Martial Artists in their respective countries or disciplines. She submitted a letter from the founder/chairman of one organization stating she had been a martial artist there for several years and was 'granted membership into our professional team through a highly rigorous process,' listing requirements including being a practicing martial artist with a black belt or higher, being 'nationally or internationally recognized,' having attained gold or silver awards in at least 10 national or international competitions, and demonstrating 'outstanding ability' to the Board of Directors. She submitted a letter from a WTKF representative asserting she had been a member since 2004, describing that membership required presenting oneself to a committee of judges who evaluate career and run theoretical and practical exams, identifying five 'esteemed martial artists' (WTKF Board Members from several countries) who served on her evaluation committee. She submitted a letter from a former WSKF board member (now working as an instructor at a WSKF Academy in Ireland) stating she was granted WSKF membership in 2015 and that he served on the evaluation committee alongside other senior WSKF officials, and that 'WSKF approved the incorporation of [the Petitioner] due to her National and International achievement along her career.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO concluded the letters did not establish eligibility. The first letter did not indicate when she became a member, and it was unclear whether the listed requirements applied to membership, team membership, or both. The author did not indicate where the organization's official membership requirements could be found, identify the Board of Directors members, or indicate whether they were recognized national/international experts. Without evidence of when she became a member, there was no basis to evaluate whether she met the stated requirements. The WTKF letter did not demonstrate that membership requires 'outstanding achievements' since it did not identify the basis on which the committee evaluates a martial artist's career, and the AAO could not determine that passing practical and theoretical exams was an 'outstanding achievement' in itself. No supporting evidence such as WTKF by-laws was submitted. The AAO reviewed the official WTKF website and found no indication that athletes may apply for individual membership—applications refer only to national federations seeking membership. The WSKF letter did not sufficiently detail the membership requirements and review procedures for individual members, and there was no supporting documentation.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The evidence was limited to a letter from the founder/chairman of an organization stating the petitioner 'performed as a judge for our competitions' and that her responsibilities as a judge were at a national level. The letter listed 23 events held between 2000 and 2014, identifying them as 'national events,' and indicated she served as a judge at the junior, elite, and elite/black belt level. He stated that to be a judge for national-level competitions, an athlete must possess a brown belt or higher and pass a written test, and that the petitioner 'was selected as a judge for our national competitions ... because of her internationally recognized track record as a martial artist and the fact that she is a respected figure in karate.' On appeal, the petitioner claimed for the first time that she is an accredited judge, instructor and examiner of WSKF and judged national and international WSKF-sponsored competitions in 2015 and 2016.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO disagreed with the Director's determination that the initial evidence was sufficient. The petitioner did not submit documentation of her judging qualification or her judging credentials from any of the referenced events, many of which were also listed as events in which she competed as an athlete. Additionally, there was a timeline inconsistency: the petitioner was only 14 years old in 2000, the year the letter asserted she began officiating national level junior competitions, while her earliest award in competition was in 2003. The AAO noted the new appeal claim about WSKF judging would need to be reviewed on remand.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO withdrew the Director's decision and remanded the matter for entry of a new decision. The AAO agreed with the petitioner that the Director's final merits analysis was insufficient because both decisions contained few references to the submitted evidence—the Director did not mention or weigh evidence related to the membership and judging criteria and simply observed that the criteria had been met. The Director's analysis rested on only two factors: that the petitioner had not demonstrated the level of competition in her tournaments was 'elite,' and a lack of evidence related to competition between 2014 and the 2017 filing date. The Director failed to adequately explain the reasons for denial as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.3(a)(1)(i) and Matter of M-P-. However, upon de novo review, the AAO also disagreed with the Director's conclusion that the petitioner satisfied three initial evidentiary criteria, finding the evidence for awards, membership, and judging was deficient and inconsistent. Because the petitioner was not put on notice of the deficiencies, the matter was remanded for further review, with the Director instructed to allow the petitioner an opportunity to submit additional evidence and, if three criteria are ultimately satisfied, to conduct a proper final merits determination.
legal_citations: Rijal v. USCIS, 772 F. Supp. 2d 1339 (W.D. Wash. 2011)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Procedural issue: The AAO found the Director failed to adequately explain the reasons for denying the petition, violating 8 C.F.R. § 103.3(a)(1)(i) and the principle from Matter of M-P-, because the final merits analysis contained few references to the submitted evidence and did not weigh evidence for the membership and judging criteria. Evidentiary inconsistencies: The record contained multiple internal inconsistencies that the petitioner failed to resolve, including medals that did not match the events claimed (2003 medals apparently from a different cup than the certificate indicated; 2005 medals indicating a different championship than claimed; 2011 medals actually being from a '4th Traditional Karate Tournament' with third/fourth place finishes rather than the claimed championship); an implausible claim of receiving multiple medals (gold, silver, and bronze) in the same individual kumite event; and a timeline inconsistency where the petitioner allegedly began judging national competitions at age 14 in 2000, three years before her earliest competition award. The AAO cited Matter of Ho requiring the petitioner to resolve inconsistencies with independent, objective evidence.
